### Cross-method evaluation of Protein 1 models

#### **Methods**
To establish a robust and unbiased comparison across the different predictive algorithms, a standardized cross-method evaluation protocol was implemented. This methodology will be consistently applied for the final selection of all subsequent proteins in this study.

First, to evaluate stereochemical and geometric quality without algorithmic bias, all generated `.pdb` files (regardless of their origin) were submitted to the universal [SWISS-MODEL Structure Assessment tool](https://swissmodel.expasy.org/assess) to extract their respective QMEANDisCo Global scores.

Second, to quantify the topological divergence between the predicted structures, pairwise 3D alignments were performed using PyMOL. To account for the discrepancy in the oligomeric states predicted by the different servers (e.g., hexamers vs. trimers) and to prevent alignment failures, the superposition was performed strictly on a single monomeric subunit (Chain A) extracted from each complex using the align command. The resulting Root Mean Square Deviation (RMSD) was recorded for each pair.

Additionally, [VoroMQA](https://bioinformatics.lt/wtsam/voromqa) was employed for third-level structural validation. It evaluates structural quality based purely on geometric criteria, using Voronoi tessellation to assess atomic packing and detect potential structural inconsistencies. The parameters used were: `Input biological assemblies`, to evaluate the complete oligomeric state and `Enable evaluation of inter-chain interfaces`, to specifically assess the quality of interactions at the subunit interfaces.

#### **Final model selection**

The universal assessment using the QMEANDisCo Global metric yielded scores of 0.61 for SWISS-MODEL, 0.50 for AlphaFold3, and 0.47 for ESMFold. While SWISS-MODEL numerically achieved the highest score, this is a well-known artifact of template-based modeling: the server forcibly truncated the highly flexible N- and C-terminal tails, evaluating strictly the highly conserved transmembrane core. In contrast, both AI algorithms modeled the full-length sequence. Consequently, their global scores were mathematically penalized by the presence of these massive intrinsically disordered regions, reflecting biological reality rather than a failure in geometric prediction.

The pairwise structural alignments in PyMOL (monomer vs. monomer), summarized in @tbl-rmsd-monomer-comparisons-orai1, revealed notable topological insights. The alignment between the two ab initio Artificial Intelligence models (AlphaFold3 vs. ESMFold) yielded the lowest structural deviation, with an RMSD of 3.27 Å across 1539 aligned atoms. However, when these AI models were compared against the homology prediction, the error increased significantly: AF3 vs. SWISS-MODEL yielded an RMSD of 4.90 Å (over 1272 atoms), and ESMFold vs. SWISS-MODEL resulted in 5.14 Å (over 1306 atoms). This geometric divergence strongly indicates that the homology model is heavily constrained by the specific crystallization conditions and multimeric twist of the *Drosophila* template. Meanwhile, the evolutionary (AF3) and linguistic (ESMFold) neural networks independently converged on a much closer topological consensus for the human sequence in its native solution state.

| Pairwise alignment       | Structural level | RMSD (Å) | Aligned atoms |
|-------------------------|------------------|---------:|--------------:|
| AlphaFold3 vs. ESMFold  | Monomer          |     3.27 |          1539 |
| AlphaFold3 vs. SWISS-MODEL | Monomer       |     4.90 |          1272 |
| ESMFold vs. SWISS-MODEL | Monomer          |     5.14 |          1306 |  

: RMSD values obtained from pairwise PyMOL alignments of monomeric structural models. {#tbl-rmsd-monomer-comparisons-orai1}

The results of the VoroMQA evaluation are summarized in the @tbl-model-comparison-ORAI1.

| Model | Global score | Interface i_score | i_energy |
|------|-------------|------------------|---------|
| Swiss-Model | 0.467 | 0.589 | -10441.9 |
| AlphaFold 3 | 0.432 | 0.584 | -10669.8 |
| ESMFold | 0.361 | 0.544 | -3540.4 |

: Comparison of the structural quality and interface scores obtained for the models generated with Swiss-Model, AlphaFold3, and ESMFold for ORAI1. {#tbl-model-comparison-ORAI1}

Although the compared models correspond to different oligomeric states (hexamer in AF3/Swiss-Model vs. trimer in ESMFold), the VoroMQA analysis allows normalization of the atomic packing quality. 

The model generated by Swiss-Model, as is shown in the @tbl-model-comparison-ORAI1, had the highest global score, which is consistent with its template-based origin. However, the AF3 model displayed the most favorable interface energy (i_energy), suggesting that it optimizes the contacts between the transmembrane helices more effectively than classical homology modeling. The ESMFold model, which represents a smaller multimeric unit (trimer), showed lower energy values overall; nevertheless, it maintained a competitive interface score (i_score), supporting the stability of the inter-chain interface even in the absence of the complete hexameric assembly.

In addition, the Local Score profiles (upper panels in @fig-voromqa-panels) shows that the highest-confidence regions (blue) occur at the same positions in all three models. Although the fourth transmembrane helix is slightly less well defined in the ESMFold model, the same trend can still be observed in the score distributions shown in the lower panels. This indicates that the transmembrane helices are correctly packed in all cases, which is consistent with the strong structural conservation of the channel.

::: {#fig-voromqa-panels layout-ncol=1}

![a) AlphaFold3 VoroMQA evaluation](/pics/ORAI1/VoroMQA/Voro_AF3.png){#a}

![b) Swiss-Model VoroMQA evaluation](/pics/ORAI1/VoroMQA/Voro_Swiss.png){#b}

![c) ESMFold VoroMQA evaluation](/pics/ORAI1/VoroMQA/Voro_ESM.png){#c}

**VoroMQA structural quality assessment for the three predicted models** (AlphaFold3, Swiss-Model and ESMFold). For each figure, the upper tracks show local structural quality and sequence annotations, while the lower plot shows the residue-wise quality score. Higher values (blue) indicate better predicted local quality, whereas lower values (red) indicate less reliable regions.
:::

Based on this analysis, the Swiss-Model structure achieves the highest global score, but for the same reasons discussed previously. In contrast, AlphaFold3 reflects the biologically realistic situation more accurately: clearly shows that the N- and C-terminal tails are flexible (which is penalized by VoroMQA).

For all these reasons, the final model selected was the one generated by AlphaFold3, shown in @fig-orai1-final. This model also includes the calcium ion, providing a more realistic representation of the physiological structure. In it, the formation of the central pore can be clearly observed, along with the correct packing of the transmembrane helices and the expected arrangement of the cytoplasmic N- and C-terminal regions. 

::: {#fig-orai1-final layout="[[1,1], [1,1]]"}

![**AlphaFold3, Side View.** Colored by chains](/pics/ORAI1/final/chains_vertical.png){#fig-a}

![**AlphaFold3, Top View.** Colored by chains](/pics/ORAI1/final/chains_poro.png){#fig-b}

![**AlphaFold3, Side View.** Colored by secondary structure](/pics/ORAI1/final/ss_vertical.png){#fig-c}

![**AlphaFold3, Top View.** Colored by secondary structure](/pics/ORAI1/final/ss_poro.png){#fig-d}

**Final AlphaFold3 model of the ORAI1 hexameric channel**. Calcium ion is represented in pink. (a) Side view of the structure colored by chains, showing the six subunits that form the channel. (b) Top (pore) view colored by chains, highlighting the symmetric arrangement of the hexamer. (c) Side view colored by secondary structure, where α-helices are shown in navy blue, β-sheets in green, and loops or disordered regions in red. (d) Top (pore) view colored by secondary structure. 
:::
